title: Listen like it counts
description: Real listening is active — in lectures and in life.
summaryPoints: Treat listening as active work: attend, process the meaning, engage
Walk into a lecture with a quick preview so your brain has hooks
Catch structure and signposts instead of transcribing word-for-word
Read the teacher's cues — repetition, emphasis, 'this is on the test'
In conversation: paraphrase, ask, and stop rehearsing your reply

content.title: Hearing isn't listening
content.body:
Your earbuds are out, the teacher is talking, sound is hitting your ears. That's **hearing** — totally automatic, requires nothing from you.

**Listening** is different. It's full attention plus actually processing what the words mean plus engaging with it. It's work, and it's a skill you can get scary good at.

The gap between the two is why you can sit through a whole lecture and remember almost none of it.

content.title: Show up with hooks
content.body:
Walk into a lecture cold and every idea is brand new — nothing to hang it on.

Spend two minutes beforehand skimming the slides or last week's notes. Now you've got **hooks**: half-familiar terms your brain can snag the new stuff onto.

Then sit somewhere you'll actually focus — usually nearer the front, away from the friend who makes you laugh. Where you sit quietly decides how much you catch.
content.imageAlt: A bullseye with a single arrow

content.prompt: Trying to write down every single word the teacher says is the best way to listen.
content.explanation: You can't think and transcribe at the same time — chasing every word means you stop processing the meaning.

content.context: Mid-lecture, the teacher says something that completely loses you.
options: Jot the exact thing that confused you so you can ask
Pretend you got it and keep nodding
Open your phone — you'll just read about it later
content.explanation: Naming the gap turns confusion into a clear question, instead of a hole that quietly grows for the rest of class.

content.title: Phone away, fully away
content.body:
Here's the brutal part: your phone doesn't even have to buzz to wreck your focus. Just **sitting on the desk**, face down, it splits your attention — a slice of your brain keeps idling on it.

Attention is a fixed budget. Multitasking doesn't expand it, it just spends it badly, and comprehension is what gets shortchanged.

So it goes in the bag. Not in your pocket, not flipped over — in the bag.

content.instructions: Tap the word that makes this advice wrong.
content.sentence: Splitting your attention in a lecture boosts how much you remember.
content.explanation: Attention is limited — divide it and comprehension drops. Single-task to actually learn.

items.text: Putting the lecture's main idea in your own words
Letting the sound wash over you while you scroll
Noting a question to ask later
Zoning out and planning to catch up someday
Watching for signposts like 'the key point is'
Copying slides down without reading them

content.title: Listen for the skeleton
content.body:
Good speakers hand you the structure out loud — you just have to catch it.

- "There are **three reasons**…" → make three slots
- "The **key point** is…" → this one matters, mark it
- "**In contrast**…" → here comes the opposite side

These signposts are a map. Follow them and you capture how the ideas fit together, not just a pile of disconnected facts.
content.imageAlt: A Cornell-style notebook with a pen

content.title: Cues that scream 'test'
content.body:
Teachers leak what they care about. Train yourself to catch the tells:

- They **repeat** something — it's important
- They slow down or get **louder** for emphasis
- They **write it on the board**
- They literally say **"this will be on the test"**

When a cue fires, mark that note. You're basically being handed the study guide in real time.

content.prompt: Match each teacher cue to what it's quietly telling you.
Which note is doing it right?
content.context: The teacher spends five minutes explaining how a recession affects jobs.
options: Every sentence the teacher said, copied as fast as possible
"Recession = less spending, so businesses cut jobs" in your words
Nothing — it sounded obvious enough to remember
content.explanation: Rewriting the main idea in your own words proves you processed it; word-for-word copying and 'I'll remember' both skip the thinking.

content.prompt: Put the active-listening routine in order.
steps: Preview the topic so you have hooks
Sit up front and stash the phone
Capture main ideas in your own words
content.explanation: Prep builds hooks, focus protects attention, capturing locks in meaning, and reviewing keeps it from leaking away.

content.title: Listening to people, not just lectures
content.body:
Same skill, higher stakes. When a friend's talking and you're already loading your reply, you've stopped listening — you're just waiting.

Try this instead:
- **Paraphrase** back: "So you're saying…" — it proves you caught it
- **Ask** a clarifying question instead of assuming
- Read their **tone and body language**, not only the words
- Don't **interrupt** — let them finish the thought

People can feel the difference instantly.

content.prompt: While a friend is talking, the best use of your brain is rehearsing what you'll say next.
content.explanation: Rehearsing your reply pulls attention off them — you miss what they actually said and the conversation turns into two monologues.

content.prompt: What's the strongest listening move here?
content.context: A friend is venting about a fight, and you're not totally sure what they're upset about.
options: Jump in with the exact same thing that happened to you
Tell them what they should do before they finish
Paraphrase it back: 'So you're saying you felt ignored?'
content.explanation: Paraphrasing checks you understood and signals you're actually with them — advice and one-upping both cut the listening short.

content.title: The lecture
content.intro: You're in a class you find painfully boring. Every choice nudges how much you actually understand. Get the bar up.

steps.situation: You sit down and your phone is in your hand.

options.text: Phone in the bag, out of reach
options.feedback: Its mere presence splits attention — gone is best.

options.text: Face down on the desk, just in case
options.feedback: Even silent and face down, it keeps skimming your focus.

options.text: Text under the desk where nobody sees
options.feedback: Now you're fully multitasking, and comprehension tanks.

steps.situation: The teacher starts and you've got a blank page.

options.text: Try to write down every single word
options.feedback: You can't think and transcribe at once — meaning slips by.

options.text: Write each main point in your own words
options.feedback: Rephrasing forces you to process the meaning.
Pure listening with zero capture leaks away fast.

steps.situation: Something the teacher says genuinely confuses you.

options.text: Jot the exact question you have to ask
options.feedback: Naming the gap turns confusion into a fixable question.

options.text: Zone out and plan to catch up later
options.feedback: 'Later' rarely comes, and the gap only grows from here.

content.outro: Boring or not, listening is a choice you make move by move — and the bar follows.
